title: Heaps / Priority Queues
description: Priority queues power top-K problems, merge-K-sorted, and Dijkstra's algorithm. Master C++'s priority_queue with max-heap default, min-heap via greater<>, and the top-K pattern.

sections.title: Max-heap with priority_queue
sections.explanation: **priority_queue<int>** is a max-heap by default — `.top()` gives the largest element. Use `.push()` to insert and `.pop()` to remove the top (returns void, like stack). Always check `.empty()` before accessing top. The underlying container is a vector, giving O(log n) push/pop and O(1) top.

exercise.prompt: Push values 10, 40, 20, 30 into a max-heap. Print the top element, pop it, then print the new top and the remaining size.

validation.message: Max-heap top is 40, after pop the next top is 30, size is 3
Use .top() to read max, .pop() to remove it, .size() for count

exercise.hint: pq.top() gives 40 (the largest). After pq.pop(), the new top is 30. pq.size() returns 3.

sections.title: Min-heap with greater<>
sections.explanation: For a min-heap, declare **priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>>**. Now `.top()` gives the smallest element. The three template params are: value type, underlying container, and comparator. This is essential for problems like merge-K-sorted-lists and Dijkstra.

exercise.prompt: Create a min-heap. Push values 15, 5, 25, 10. Print the top (smallest), pop it, print the new top, and the size.

validation.message: Min-heap top is 5, after pop next is 10, size is 3
Use greater<int> as the comparator to create a min-heap

exercise.hint: Use greater<int> as the third template argument. top() gives 5 (the smallest), then 10 after popping.

sections.title: Heap with pairs
sections.explanation: **priority_queue** works with **pair<int,T>** — it compares by `.first`, then `.second`. For a min-heap of pairs, use `priority_queue<pair<int,string>, vector<pair<int,string>>, greater<>>`. This pattern is used in Dijkstra (distance, node) and scheduling problems.

exercise.prompt: Create a min-heap of (priority, task) pairs. Push (3, "low"), (1, "high"), (2, "medium"). Pop all elements and print them in priority order as "task:priority".

validation.message: Min-heap pops in ascending priority: high(1), medium(2), low(3)
Use greater<> for min-heap, top() to read, pop() to remove

exercise.hint: pq.empty() checks the loop. pq.top() reads the smallest pair. pq.pop() removes it.

sections.title: Top-K pattern
sections.explanation: To find the K largest elements, use a **min-heap of size K**: push each element, and if the heap exceeds size K, pop the smallest. The heap retains the K largest values. This runs in O(n log K), which is better than O(n log n) full sort when K is small. For K smallest, use a max-heap of size K instead.

exercise.prompt: Find the 3 largest elements from {7, 2, 9, 1, 5, 8, 3} using a min-heap of size K. Print them space-separated (ascending order is fine).

validation.message: The 3 largest are 7, 8, 9 (printed in ascending order from the min-heap)
Use a min-heap, push elements, and pop when size exceeds K

exercise.hint: Push each element with minPQ.push(x). When size > k, pop the smallest. The remaining K elements are the largest.
